title: Nail Polish on Silk
timeEstimate: Pro-only — do not improvise at home
stainChemistry: Nail polish is a tough plastic film with concentrated pigment, designed to dry into a hard layer that doesn't come off easily. The chemistry that takes polish off — acetone, drugstore polish remover — is exactly what damages silk. Acetone destroys silk's hand and creates permanent rings. Non-acetone removers bleed silk dye. Even rubbing alcohol strips silk color where it lands. Water alone leaves rings on silk. There is no safe home solvent for nail polish on silk.
whyThisWorks: A pro spotter applies a controlled ester solvent with tight blotting, a clean rinse, and an acid neutralization — all under moisture discipline silk needs and home can't safely deliver. The amount of solvent needed to soften the polish is the same amount that damages silk if applied wrong. Home protocol is to blot any wet polish fast, leave the dry film alone, and get to a specialist.
scienceNote: Mechanism (pro-facing): polish is nitrocellulose film former plus plasticizers plus pigment in ester solvents (ethyl acetate, butyl acetate, sometimes acetone). Pro uses amyl acetate at controlled concentration with timed dwell plus immediate NSD rinse plus acetic acid neutralization — chemistry that softens nitrocellulose without damaging silk fibroin.
defaultAssumption: Silk + nail polish = pro IMMEDIATELY. Customer has no amyl acetate or pro spotting experience. Default recommendation = take it to a silk specialist with NOTHING applied at home.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Amyl acetate, white cloth, hidden seam
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply amyl acetate to a hidden seam for 30 seconds. Check for sheen loss, ring formation, or color bleed. Any of those = stop, refer to silk damage protocol.

spottingProtocol.dwellTime: 5–10 minutes total, refresh cloth each pass
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply to film with white cloth, tight blotting motion, concentric passes from edge inward. Refresh cloth each pass. Do NOT flood silk.
Sponge gently with minimum moisture, blot dry between passes. Removes residual pigment without flooding silk.
Dropper for pH restoration. Blot. Air-dry flat on absorbent padding, no heat, no hanging wet.

steps: 1. Hidden-seam amyl acetate test — abort on any sheen / ring / bleed.
2. Amyl acetate tight-tamp blotting, concentric passes.
3. Cold NSD sponge with minimum moisture.
4. Diluted acetic acid for pH restoration.
warnings: NEVER acetone on silk — destroys hand + permanent rings
NEVER nail polish remover (acetone OR non-acetone) on silk — uncontrolled esters
NEVER isopropyl alcohol on silk — strips silk dyes
NEVER flood silk with water — permanent ring formation
Always fiber-test amyl acetate at hidden seam first

homeSolutions.instruction: Nail polish on silk is the rare combination where NO home solvent is safe. Acetone destroys silk's hand and creates permanent rings. "Non-acetone" removers contain uncontrolled ester concentrations that bleed silk dyes. Rubbing alcohol strips silk color wherever applied. Water alone leaves permanent rings on silk and does nothing to nitrocellulose anyway. The cotton playbook doesn't transfer. Best move: leave the stain alone, take to a silk-safe specialist within 24 hours.

homeSolutions.agent: If Wet — Blot Once Gently, Then Stop
homeSolutions.instruction: If the polish is still liquid, press a dry white cloth STRAIGHT DOWN on the pool, outside edges toward the center. Lift straight up — no wiping, no smearing. 2–3 light dabs to pick up surface liquid, then stop. Do NOT add water, do NOT rub, do NOT press deeper into the weave. Bulk surface only.

homeSolutions.agent: If Dry — Leave It Completely Alone
homeSolutions.instruction: Hardened polish on silk is EASIER for a pro to handle than disturbed polish. Do NOT pick at it with a fingernail. Do NOT scrape with a knife or card — scraping tears silk fibroin. Do NOT attempt to soften with anything (heat, water, alcohol). Silk is fragile; leave the polish in place until the cleaner can apply controlled amyl acetate.

Not every dry cleaner can handle silk. Call ahead: "Do you treat silk with amyl acetate for nail polish?" If they don't understand the question, try another cleaner. When you bring it in, tell them: "Nail polish on silk — [wet still / dried]. NOTHING applied at home. No acetone, no remover, no alcohol, no water, no scrubbing." That handoff matters — pros can go straight to controlled amyl acetate spotting with confidence the silk hasn't been compromised.

steps: 1. Silk + nail polish = pro IMMEDIATELY. NO home solvents.
2. If wet: 2–3 light dabs straight down with white cloth. Stop.
3. If dry: leave alone. Do not pick, scrape, or soften.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Always stop after the initial blot. Any home solvent on silk creates new damage worse than the polish.
warnings: NEVER acetone on silk — permanent damage
NEVER nail polish remover (any kind) on silk
NEVER alcohol / IPA on silk — strips dyes
NEVER water on silk for polish — permanent rings
NEVER scrape dried polish — tears fibroin
NEVER improvise solvent at home — pro-only

neverDo: Never acetone on silk — destroys hand + permanent rings
Never nail polish remover (acetone or non-acetone) — uncontrolled esters
Never isopropyl alcohol on silk — strips dyes
Never water flooding on silk — permanent rings
Never scrape dried polish — tears silk
fiberSensitivities: Silk fibroin damaged by acetone + most aggressive solvents
Silk dyes stripped by isopropyl alcohol
Silk rings permanently from water alone
Amyl acetate is the only known silk-safe solvent for nail polish — pro-only application

materialWarnings: Pro IMMEDIATELY — no safe home solvent
Don't pick or scrape dried polish

escalation.when: Always — silk + nail polish is pro-only IMMEDIATELY. The cleaner's best outcome is the one where you bring it in with NOTHING applied at home.
escalation.whatToTell: "Nail polish on silk, [wet still / dried], age [hours / days]. NOTHING applied at home. No acetone, no remover, no alcohol, no water, no scrubbing."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner specializing in silk (call ahead — confirm amyl acetate spotting + nitrocellulose-on-silk experience)

contextQuestions: Type of silk — charmeuse / chiffon / habotai / dupioni?
Has anyone tried anything yet (acetone, remover, alcohol)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Pre-applied acetone or non-acetone remover = silk likely already damaged where you applied it (rings, dye bleed, sheen loss). Pre-applied IPA = silk dye likely already stripped. Pre-applied water = ring already present. Disclose honestly — changes pro plan. Dark polish (red, black, purple) leaves more pigment ghost; pro will set expectations.
